postData.contentHtml:
The final day of a World Surfing League (WSL) event in Raglan, New Zealand, took an unexpected turn when Australian water photographer Ed Sloane sustained a 'wildlife injury' to his foot. The incident occurred on Monday morning while Sloane was capturing a surfing heat from within the water.

Following the incident, WSL Commissioner Renato Hickel quickly issued a 'code red,' immediately halting the competition and clearing the water. Sloane was promptly transported to a local hospital. Hickel later confirmed via Instagram that Sloane was in stable condition and, despite suffering 'minor, small puncture wounds,' was in 'great spirits.'

Officials are still investigating the exact nature of the animal responsible for the bite, with possibilities ranging from a shark to a sea lion, though the latter is considered more likely. Sloane himself expressed gratitude for the rapid response from the water patrol, medical team, and all supporting teams, adding that he 'loves this place and can't wait to watch an epic Finals Day.'

The competition resumed later the same day at 1:05 p.m. NZST, following consultations with surfers and stakeholders. To ensure the safety of all participants, heightened wildlife surveillance measures were implemented, including the deployment of jet skis, drones, and dedicated spotters. The incident, while momentarily startling, concluded with positive sentiment regarding Sloane's recovery and the event's professional handling.
postData.title: Wildlife Interruption: Photographer Bitten at New Zealand Surf Event
postData.summary: A World Surfing League event in Raglan, New Zealand, saw an unexpected halt when an Australian water photographer suffered a 'wildlife injury' to his foot. The competition resumed with enhanced safety measures after the photographer was reported to be in good spirits.

sources.title: New Zealand surfing event halted after water photographer bitten
